items.foco: Causes of pathological jaundice in <24 hours
items.title: Pathological jaundice in <24 hours
items.contentHtml:
Causes of pathological jaundice in <24 hours

  • Hemolytic disease due to Rh incompatibility (maternal anti-D antibodies) and glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency are classic causes of visible jaundice in the first 24 hours of life.
  • Other etiologies: hereditary spherocytosis, pyruvate kinase deficiency, congenital infections (TORCH) and internal bleeding (cephalohematoma).
  • Any jaundice that appears before 24 hours is considered pathological until proven otherwise; requires immediate measurement of total and fractionated serum bilirubin.

items.title: Risk factors for kernicterus
items.contentHtml:
Risk factors for kernicterus

  • Prematurity (<37 weeks), hemolysis (Rh incompatibility, glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency), perinatal hypoxia, metabolic acidosis and hypoalbuminemia decrease the binding of bilirubin to albumin.
  • Hypoglycemia competes for albumin and displaces free bilirubin, increasing the risk of neurotoxicity.
  • kernicterus (deposition of unconjugated bilirubin in the basal ganglia) is prevented by identifying risk factors and applying phototherapy or exchange transfusion according to threshold levels.

items.title: Levels for exchange transfusion
items.contentHtml:
Bilirubin levels for exchange transfusion

  • In newborns ≥35 weeks, exchange transfusion is indicated when total serum bilirubin exceeds the 95th percentile of the Bhutani curve adjusted for hour of life and risk factors.
  • For newborns with risk factors (hemolysis, asphyxia, acidosis), the threshold is lower: approximately 4-6 mg/dL below the 95th percentile.
  • In premature infants <35 weeks, specific tables from the American Academy of Pediatrics are used; Example: 24-hour neonate with 15 mg/dL may require exchange transfusion if there is active hemolysis.

items.foco: Phototherapy: indication according to bilirubin and age
items.title: Phototherapy indication
items.contentHtml:
Phototherapy: indication according to bilirubin and age

  • Phototherapy is started when the total serum bilirubin exceeds the 75th percentile of the Bhutani curve for the gestational age and hours of life of the newborn.
  • Example: 48-hour healthy term neonate with bilirubin 15 mg/dL (75th-95th percentile) requires phototherapy; If it exceeds 18 mg/dL (95th percentile), it is considered intensive phototherapy.
  • Intensive phototherapy uses irradiance ≥30 µW/cm²/nm in the blue spectrum (425-475 nm) and maximum exposed body surface.

items.foco: Jaundice due to ABO vs Rh incompatibility
items.title: ABO vs Rh incompatibility
items.contentHtml:
Jaundice due to ABO vs Rh incompatibility

  • The ABO incompatibility (mother O, child A or B) is more frequent, it causes mild-moderate jaundice, with direct Coombs test frequently negative or weakly positive.
  • The Rh incompatibility (mother Rh negative, child Rh positive) produces severe hemolysis, direct Coombs positive strong, and requires prophylaxis with anti-D immunoglobulin in the mother.
  • In Rh incompatibility, jaundice appears in <24 hours and bilirubin levels can quickly reach the exchange transfusion threshold.

items.foco: G6PD deficiency as a hemolytic cause
items.title: G6PD deficiency
items.contentHtml:
Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency as a hemolytic cause

  • Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency (linked to the early with variable anemia.
  • The diagnosis is confirmed with measurement of enzyme activity; If suspected, start intensive phototherapy early and avoid triggers.

items.foco: Differentiation between direct and indirect bilirubin
items.title: Direct vs indirect bilirubin
items.contentHtml:
Differentiation between direct and indirect bilirubin

  • indirect bilirubin (unconjugated, insoluble in water) is the neurotoxic form; it is elevated in hemolysis, liver immaturity and Crigler-Najjar syndrome.
  • Direct bilirubin (conjugated, water-soluble) is elevated in cholestasis; a value >2 mg/dL or >20% of total bilirubin indicates obstructive jaundice or hepatocellular jaundice.
  • Differentiation is key: if direct bilirubin is elevated, phototherapy is less effective and bile duct atresia or neonatal hepatitis must be ruled out.

items.foco: Jaundice due to bile duct atresia
items.title: Bile duct atresia
items.contentHtml:
Jaundice due to bile duct atresia

  • Bile duct atresia is the most common cause of surgical neonatal obstructive jaundice; It presents with elevated direct bilirubin, acholia and persistent choluria after the second week of life.
  • The diagnosis is suspected with abdominal ultrasound (triangular cord sign) and confirmed with intraoperative cholangiography.
  • The treatment of choice is Kasai portoenterostomy, ideal before 60 days of life; Delay worsens liver prognosis.

items.title: Warning signs
items.contentHtml:
Alarm signs in neonatal jaundice

  • Jaundice in the first 24 hours, total bilirubin > 95th percentile on Bhutani curve, increase >5 mg/dL/day, direct bilirubin >2 mg/dL, and signs of kernicterus (lethargy, hypotonia, weak suction, opisthotonus).
  • Other signs: paleness (anemia), hepatosplenomegaly, vomiting. bilious, acholia, choluria, bulging fontanelle or seizures.
  • Any warning sign requires urgent evaluation with total and fractionated serum bilirubin, hematocrit, reticulocyte count and direct Coombs test.

items.title: Bhutani curve
items.contentHtml:
Bhutani curve and risk percentiles

  • The Bhutani curve (American Academy of Pediatrics nomogram) plots total serum bilirubin by hours of life for newborns ≥35 weeks.
  • The risk percentiles are: low risk (<40th percentile), intermediate risk low (40-75th percentile), high intermediate risk (75-95th percentile) and high risk (>95th percentile).
  • The 75th percentile is the threshold for initiating phototherapy, and the 95th percentile for considering exchange transfusion, adjusting according to risk factors (hemolysis, asphyxia, acidosis, hypoalbuminemia).

items.foco: Hypoglycemia as an aggravating factor of kernicterus
items.title: Hypoglycemia and kernicterus
items.contentHtml:
Hypoglycemia as an aggravating factor of kernicterus

  • Hypoglycemia competes with bilirubin for binding sites on albumin, increasing the free fraction of unconjugated bilirubin and facilitating its passage through the blood-brain barrier.
  • In neonates with hypoglycemia and hyperbilirubinemia, the risk of kernicterus increases even with moderately elevated bilirubin levels.
  • Management includes aggressive correction of hypoglycemia (intravenous glucose) and early intensive phototherapy to reduce free bilirubin.

items.title: Physiological jaundice duration
items.contentHtml:
Normal duration of physiological jaundice

  • The physiological jaundice of the full-term newborn appears after 24 hours of life, reaches its peak between day 3 and 5 (total bilirubin <12-15 mg/dL) and resolves spontaneously in 1-2 weeks.
  • In premature infants, the peak is later (day 5-7) and the duration can may last up to 3-4 weeks due to the immaturity of hepatic glucuronidation.
  • If jaundice persists for more than 2 weeks in a full-term newborn or 3 weeks in a premature newborn, it is considered prolonged and requires a study of pathological causes (direct bilirubin, thyroid function, infection).
